file.content:
### Free path: intrusive LIFO free-stack

Freeing must be cheap for many freers and allocation must be
O(1) with no searching (a per-buffer available-flag makes free
trivial but alloc a scan). The free operation is itself the
marking: push the buffer onto the pool's free-stack.

- **Intrusive** — a freed buffer's header holds the next-link
  as an offset; the free-list needs no extra storage.
- **LIFO on purpose** — we think a stack beats a free-ring on
  cache behavior: FIFO cycles through every buffer (maximum
  working set), LIFO reuses the most-recently-freed few, and
  the small working set also helps the TLB. The full
  same-thread malloc-style hot-reuse win is diluted because
  the freer and allocator are different cores; the
  working-set effect stands regardless.
- **Single-popper Treiber stack** — free = CAS the head to
  your buffer's offset (MPSC push side: any thread or process
  frees into any pool); alloc = the one owning allocator pops
  the head. A single popper eliminates classic Treiber ABA:
  no node leaves the list under a competing pop, pushes just
  retry the CAS.
- **Validated pops** — head and next-links are untrusted;
  the popper bounds/alignment-checks each offset and caps
  pops per attempt, so a corrupt peer can lose buffers or
  cycle the list but never make the allocator read outside
  the pool.
- **CAS note** — the free-stack is the first structure to
  need CAS; the ring protocol itself stays load/store-only,
  so the atomic floor rises only for pool users.

### Pool topology and phasing

- **One owning allocator per pool** (invariant, phase 1) — a
  single thread allocates; any party frees. Per-thread pools
  are the performance default and satisfy this trivially.
- **Shared pools already exist in phase 1** — shared for
  reading, forwarding, and freeing; only allocation is
  single-owner.
- **Shared allocation (phase 2)** — per-thread pools cost
  memory; N threads sharing one pool's capacity needs
  multi-popper alloc, which needs an ABA-proof head:
  pack `(offset, generation)` in one `AtomicU64` and CAS
  both together. A contained per-pool variant (opt-in flag),
  not a redesign; hot paths keep the cheap single-popper
  version. Heterogeneity mitigates meanwhile: a small private
  hot-path pool plus a big shared fallback pool.
- **MPSC ring (future sibling)** — multi-producer queues need
  a different ring protocol (CAS-claimed producer index,
  per-slot sequence state); it slots in as a sibling
  primitive under the same descriptor layer. The endpoint
  claims word should model role slots, not a single producer
  bit, so N producer claims fit later without a layout
  rethink.

### Usage model: roles and buffer lifecycle

The user-facing contract — who may do what, per object and
per buffer state — lives in the README:
[Usage model](../README.md#usage-model-roles-and-buffer-lifecycle).
The tests exercise exactly what it permits. Summary: ring =
one producer + one consumer; pool = one allocator + any
freers; a buffer is always in exactly one state
(free / allocated / in-flight [vacant until descriptor
queues] / freed) with one permitted toucher; "send" this
cycle means moving the `BufSlot`.

### Overflow FIFO (future)

When a queue's ring is Full, the sender appends the message to
a pending FIFO instead of failing — "send" then always
succeeds while pool memory lasts.

- **Intrusive, zero-allocation** — the FIFO links through the
  same embedded next-link offset the free-stack uses. A
  buffer is on at most one list at a time (free stack,
  pending FIFO, in flight in a ring, held by an owner), so
  one link field serves every state.
- **Sender-private** — the pending list belongs to the
  producer endpoint: head + tail offsets for O(1) append, no
  shared mutation, no CAS. Order discipline: the producer
  drains the FIFO oldest-first before ring-sending anything
  new, or FIFO order breaks. Draining happens on subsequent
  send attempts and/or an explicit flush.
- **Validated traversal** — the links live in shared pool
  memory a peer can scribble; the drainer bounds- and
  alignment-checks each offset as the free-stack popper does.
- **Naturally bounded** — messages come from pools, so the
  FIFO cannot outgrow total pool capacity; backpressure
  reappears as allocation failure rather than queue Full.

### Prior art: iceoryx2

[iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2) is a
Rust-native zero-copy IPC library implementing most of this
layer: publishers loan uninitialized samples from a pool
allocator, write in place, and send later or never (get/send
decoupled); receivers get offsets they resolve against their
own mappings; events, request-response, and multi-producer
patterns exist. Study its loan/send API and pool-offset
machinery before implementing the pools — its lessons are
cheaper stolen than rediscovered.

What keeps this project distinct from it:

- iceoryx2 is service-oriented middleware (discovery, naming,
  configuration) for Linux/macOS/Windows/QNX-class platforms;
  this crate is a small set of composable primitives.
- No `no_std` / load-store-only floor — our ring reaches
  `thumbv6m`, with CAS confined to the pool layer.
- Trust posture: our attach-validated
  hostile-peer-cannot-cause-UB discipline versus cooperating
  participants within a framework.

### Open questions

- **Pool-id allocation** — coordinator-assigned versus derived
  from the shm object's identity.
- **Setup plane** — how a process discovers and maps a pool it
  hasn't seen: pre-arranged at init (embedded-friendly,
  allocation-free after startup) versus fd-passing over a
  Unix socket (Linux-friendly, dynamic); likely both, as
  profiles.
- **Message header shape** — minimum provenance is
  `(pool id, offset)`; whether length or a type tag joins it
  is undecided. Settle this early — the embedded next-link
  is load-bearing for three states (free-stack, pending
  FIFO, future lists), so the link, provenance, and any
  length/type tag should be laid out together before the
  first pool implementation lands.

## Resolved questions

- **Cross-process trust** — resolved in 0.3.0-4: the
  all-atomic header plus the geometry snapshot mean a hostile
  peer cannot cause UB, only garbage messages (still valid
  `T`s — zerocopy guarantees representation, not sense) or
  spurious Full/Empty. `attach` remains `unsafe` for the
  mapping-liveness contract, not for peer trust.
- **`split()` once-guard** — direction chosen: an
  endpoint-claims word in the header (CAS to claim the
  producer / consumer role, second claimant gets an error),
  which also catches in-process double-attach; costs a
  layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`). Promoted to a
  `## Todo` entry in [todo.md](todo.md); the documented
  contract stands until then.

## In Progress

**feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring**

Getting a pool buffer must not imply sending it, and the
ring alone fuses allocation, queue position, and
publication. Descriptors — small `(pool id, buffer offset)`
values sent through the existing SPSC ring — separate them,
so any pool message travels over any queue. This cycle
builds the in-process slice: the descriptor type, pool ids
via a per-process registry, and guard ↔ descriptor
conversion; cross-process setup (mapping exchange, pool-id
coordination) stays design
[details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).

- 0.7.0-0 chore: open descriptor queue cycle (current)
- 0.7.0-1 docs: settle descriptor design questions —
  descriptor shape (offset vs index, width), pool-id
  source, resolve-side validation, the unsafe ownership
  contract for guard ↔ descriptor
- 0.7.0-2 feat: pool registry + descriptor resolve
- 0.7.0-3 test: descriptor protocol tests (round-trip,
  cross-thread free, hostile descriptors)
- 0.7.0-4 feat: demo descriptor flow ring + pool
- 0.7.0 feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring
  (close-out)

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Descriptor queues: carry `(pool id, buffer offset)`
   descriptors over the existing SPSC ring so any message
   travels over any queue; per-process pool registry to
   resolve ids
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).
2. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
3. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Descriptor queues: carry `(pool id, buffer offset)`
   descriptors over the existing SPSC ring so any message
   travels over any queue; per-process pool registry to
   resolve ids
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).
2. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
3. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- docs: messaging layer design (pools + queues) [[6]]
- feat: message pool allocator [[7]]
- feat: ring + pool demo binary [[8]]
- feat: demo alloc/free perf loops [[9]]
- feat: demo cpu-pinned placement variants [[10]]

Scope note baked into the problem statement: this cycle is the **in-process slice** — descriptor type, per-process pool registry, guard↔descriptor conversion, composed demo.   (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

content.text:
`-1` is marked current. This commit's work is settling the design, so before I write anything into `ring-buffer-design.md`, here's the proposal — four questions, each with my recommendation. This doubles as the walkthrough you wanted.

**1. Descriptor shape: index, not byte offset — `Desc { pool_id: u32, buf_idx: u32 }` (8 bytes, POD).**
The design doc says "(pool id, buffer offset)", but the doc's real requirement is "offsets only, everywhere" — meaning *no pointers* in shared structures, because each process maps the pool at a different address. A buffer **index** satisfies that just as well as a byte offset, and it's strictly simpler: it's what the free-stack and `BufSlot.idx` already use, validation is one comparison (`idx < buf_count`), and the receiver computes the address as `bufs + idx * buf_size` from its own attached geometry. A byte offset would need bounds *and* divisibility checks to prove it lands exactly on a buffer boundary. Both u32s match the pool's existing u32 index space; 8 bytes rides easily in a 64-byte ring slot.

**2. Pool-id source: assigned by a per-process registry at register time.**
The open question was coordinator-assigned vs derived-from-shm-identity — that's the *cross-process* question, and it stays open. In-process, the registry just hands out the next table slot: `registry.register(pool) -> PoolId`. The registry is fixed-capacity (const-generic array, no_std-friendly, no allocation), holding one `Pool` handle per id. That handle is a resolver/freer handle, which the pool contract already permits — "at most one attached handle allocates, any handle may free" — so the allocating thread keeps its own `Pool` while the registry holds another over the same region.

**3. Resolve-side validation: full, always.**
A descriptor arriving through shared memory is untrusted (same posture as ring geometry): unknown `pool_id` → error; `buf_idx >= buf_count` → error; `T` geometry mismatch → same `check_type` panic as `alloc` (programming error, not hostile input). A hostile descriptor can produce a garbage-but-valid `T` or a double-free-shaped protocol violation — never UB from the resolve itself.

**4. The ownership contract — this is the one real `unsafe`:**
- `registry.into_desc(slot: BufSlot<T>) -> Desc` — **safe**. Consumes the guard (identifying the pool by the guard's header address, O(N) over a tiny table), returns the descriptor. Ownership doesn't vanish — it's now *carried by the descriptor*, exactly the "in-flight" buffer state the usage model reserved.
- `unsafe registry.resolve::<T>(desc: Desc) -> Result<BufSlot<T>, Error>` — **unsafe**, and this is the honest core: resolving mints a guard that acts as `&mut T`. The types can't prevent you from resolving the same descriptor twice (two owners, aliased `&mut`) or resolving a descriptor you invented. The caller's contract: *this descriptor came from `into_desc` and is being resolved exactly once, ownership transferred through a channel with proper ordering* (the ring's commit→reserve Release/Acquire provides that). All the bounds validation in #3 still runs — `unsafe` covers only what validation can't check: uniqueness of ownership.

The flow then reads: `pool.alloc` → write → `registry.into_desc` → ring `reserve_slot::<Desc>`/commit → … → ring reserve/release → `registry.resolve` → read → `free()` (or forward it over another queue — that's the "any message over any queue" payoff).

One deliberate deferral: the design doc's *in-buffer* provenance header ("every buffer begins with `(pool id, offset)`") is not needed while provenance travels in the descriptor itself, and the pool already landed without it. I'd record that as explicitly deferred rather than silently dropped.

If this matches your understanding — or after we've hashed out whatever doesn't — I'll write it into `ring-buffer-design.md` as the `-1` docs commit. What would you like to dig into?

content.text:
Re-evaluated from scratch. The core (index-based 8-byte `Desc`, per-process registry, safe `into_desc` / unsafe `resolve`) held up, but the deeper pass found one real design hole and two refinements worth making. The delta:

**1. The registry can't soundly hold a second `Pool` handle — hole fixed with a derived view.**
My sketch had the registry holding a `Pool` obtained via `Pool::attach` on the same region the allocator's `Pool::init` already exclusively borrows. In-process that's either a borrow-checker error or (via a saved raw pointer) exactly the Stacked-Borrows retag hazard `pool.rs` already warns about — `init` takes the region pointer *exactly once* by design. The fix is to derive the second handle from the first instead of from the region: `pool.resolver() -> PoolResolver` (a `&self` method copying the header ref, buffer base, and geometry — same provenance as the pool's own pointers, so no new aliasing). `PoolResolver` is the non-allocating view: it can mint guards from validated indices and identify its pool by header address; it's `Send + Sync` (its only shared-memory touch is the free CAS, already MPSC-safe), so one registry of resolvers is shared by plain `&` across both demo threads while the allocator keeps its unique `Pool`. This also composes unchanged with cross-process later — a resolver derived from an `attach`ed handle works identically.

**2. Geometry mismatch on resolve is `Err`, not the `alloc`-style panic.**
`alloc::<T>` panics on `T`-vs-pool geometry mismatch because you chose `T` against your own pool — a programming error. But `resolve::<T>(desc)` checks `T` against *whichever pool the descriptor names*, and the descriptor is untrusted input. A hostile peer choosing which pool gets compared must not be able to choose a panic (that's a DoS lever, against the hostile-peer posture). So resolve's failures are all `Err`: unknown pool id, index out of range, geometry mismatch. `unsafe` on resolve then covers only the one thing validation can't check — ownership uniqueness: *this desc came from `into_desc`, arrived over a happens-before-establishing channel (the ring's commit→reserve gives this), and is resolved exactly once.*

**3. `into_desc` returns the guard back on failure.**
`registry.into_desc(slot)` consumes the guard; if the slot's pool isn't registered, returning a bare error would strand the buffer (guards leak on drop, by design). So the error variant carries the `BufSlot` back: `Result<Desc, (BufSlot<T>, Error)>`-shaped. No leak paths introduced by the new API.

Two confirmations from the re-check worth stating: `Desc` **must** be plain copyable data — `FromBytes` means the ring consumer literally mints one from shared bytes, so there's no point making it a move-only token; ownership discipline lives entirely in resolve's contract. And the in-buffer provenance header stays deferred cleanly: in this design the descriptor *is* the message's travel form (forwarding re-sends the desc), so no flow exists where a buffer travels without its provenance — the deferral costs nothing until someone invents one, and adding it later is a pool `layout_version` bump.

The ladder holds as written (`-2` grows `Pool::resolver()` but the title still fits). If this version matches your understanding, I'll write it into `ring-buffer-design.md` as the `-1` docs commit.

message.content:
Makes some sense, but some observations/constraints:
- In the simplest case an embedded system with no virtual or shared memory,
  I think the descriptor can boil down to a pool_id maybe the buf_idx could both be pointers.
- We haven't touched on message formats other than using Google Zerocopy transmut
  capabilities, but in the real world with multiple messages each message is
  going to have an id which will drive a match statement or .. to know which
  message we have. Shouldn't matter at this level but a demo use of this will
  need something like that.
- Not now but I'm wondering how trait's might be use to allow different usage styles,
  maybe a trait could handle the boiler plate in a "casting" a buffer to an Instance
  of a message?

content.text:
All three observations are sound — here's how each lands in the design, and then I've written the `-1` doc. 

**Embedded pointer descriptors**: agreed that's the degenerate form — no MMU, one trust domain, the descriptor could just be a flattened guard (pool ptr + buf ptr) and resolve becomes a cast. I'm keeping `(pool_id, buf_idx)` as the *canonical* form though, for two reasons: a pointer can't be validated against anything (a scribbled pointer descriptor is UB on deref, where id+index degrades to `Err` — the crate's corruption posture), and the id form's resolve cost is already near-pointer-cheap (array index + two bounds checks, no search). The pointer profile gets recorded as a future opt-in, which connects to your traits point. Your observation also improved the design: `into_desc` now takes the `PoolId` explicitly (the sender got it from `register`), making it O(1) with a header-identity check catching mispairing, instead of my O(N) scan.

**Message ids / dispatch**: agreed, and it's cleanly out of this layer — the descriptor is deliberately type-agnostic; the tag that drives the receiver's `match` is *payload*-level (first word of the message, or `TryFromBytes` tagged enums). The design doc's open "message header shape" question gets half-settled: provenance travels in the descriptor, type tag is payload-level, in-buffer header deferred. The demo will carry a minimal first-word tag so the flow looks real.

**Traits for usage styles**: parked in `## Ideas` — a `Message` trait (const `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds) hiding the cast boilerplate, with a possible transport seam the pointer-descriptor profile could slot in behind.

Now writing the design into the notes:

input.new_string:
### Descriptor and registry design (0.7.0)

The in-process slice as designed for the 0.7.0 cycle;
cross-process setup (mapping exchange, pool-id
coordination) remains in [Open questions](#open-questions).

- **`Desc { pool_id: u32, buf_idx: u32 }`** — 8 bytes, POD
  (zerocopy derives), so it rides any queue as an ordinary
  message. A buffer *index*, not a byte offset: the
  offsets-only rule bars pointers (per-process mappings),
  and an index is equally position-independent while
  matching what the free-stack and guards already speak;
  validation is one bounds check where a byte offset would
  also need a buffer-boundary divisibility check.
- **`PoolRegistry`** — per-process, fixed capacity
  (const-generic array; no_std, zero allocation).
  `register(resolver) -> PoolId` assigns the next slot
  index; phase 1 has no unregister, so ids never dangle.
- **`Pool::resolver() -> PoolResolver`** — a non-allocating
  view (header ref, buffer base, geometry) derived from the
  existing handle, so no second region borrow and no
  Stacked Borrows retag hazard (`init` takes the region
  pointer exactly once). Send + Sync: resolving mints
  guards from validated indices, and the only
  shared-memory mutation is the guards' free CAS, already
  any-thread. Cross-process later, the same view derives
  from an `attach`ed handle.
- **`into_desc(slot, pool_id)`** — safe, O(1): checks the
  guard's header address against the id's registry entry
  (catches id/pool mispairing); consumes the guard, and
  ownership travels on in the descriptor (the usage
  model's "in-flight" state). The error side hands the
  guard back, so a miss cannot leak the buffer.
- **`unsafe resolve::<T>(desc) -> Result<BufSlot<T>, _>`**
  — every failure is an `Err`, never a panic: unknown pool
  id, index out of range, `T` geometry mismatch. The
  geometry case differs from `alloc` (which panics)
  because the descriptor selects which pool gets compared
  — untrusted input must not select a panic. `unsafe`
  covers the one thing validation cannot check —
  ownership: the caller promises the desc came from
  `into_desc`, arrived over a channel establishing
  happens-before (ring commit → reserve qualifies), and is
  resolved exactly once.
- **`Desc` is plain data on purpose** — `FromBytes` means a
  receiver mints one from shared bytes anyway, so a
  move-only ownership token would be theater; the
  discipline lives in resolve's contract.
- **In-buffer provenance deferred** — the descriptor is the
  message's travel form (forwarding re-sends it), so no
  flow carries a buffer without its provenance; adding an
  in-buffer header later is a pool `layout_version` bump.
- **Type dispatch is payload-level** — the descriptor stays
  type-agnostic. Multiple message types over one queue
  need a receiver-readable tag: a first-word id driving a
  match (demo-minimal), `TryFromBytes` tagged enums, or a
  future `Message` trait hiding the cast boilerplate (see
  todo Ideas).
- **Single-address-space profile (future)** — with no MMU
  and one trust domain, a descriptor could carry pointers
  (a flattened guard): zero-lookup resolve, but nothing to
  validate against — a scribbled pointer descriptor is UB
  on use, where id + index degrades to an `Err`. The
  canonical form stays `(pool id, buf idx)`; a pointer
  profile could arrive later behind the same trait seam as
  other usage styles.

### Free path: intrusive LIFO free-stack

  embedded next-link — the link is load-bearing for three
  states (free-stack, pending FIFO, future lists), so they
  must be laid out together when that header lands.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring**

Getting a pool buffer must not imply sending it, and the
ring alone fuses allocation, queue position, and
publication. Descriptors — small `(pool id, buffer offset)`
values sent through the existing SPSC ring — separate them,
so any pool message travels over any queue. This cycle
builds the in-process slice: the descriptor type, pool ids
via a per-process registry, and guard ↔ descriptor
conversion; cross-process setup (mapping exchange, pool-id
coordination) stays design
[details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).

- 0.7.0-0 chore: open descriptor queue cycle (done)
- 0.7.0-1 docs: settle descriptor design questions —
  descriptor shape (offset vs index, width), pool-id
  source, resolve-side validation, the unsafe ownership
  contract for guard ↔ descriptor (current)
- 0.7.0-2 feat: pool registry + descriptor resolve
- 0.7.0-3 test: descriptor protocol tests (round-trip,
  cross-thread free, hostile descriptors)
- 0.7.0-4 feat: demo descriptor flow ring + pool
- 0.7.0 feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring
  (close-out)

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- feat: demo cpu-pinned placement variants [[10]]

message.content:
I need an overview/outline level of the detailed version you have now,
Here is an example:
- A process may have one or more pools
- Not all pools in a process need to be in shared memory
- Messages that are to be sent to other processes must be 
  be from a pool in shared memory.
- Any process receiving a message from another process may free the message
  and it will be returned to the appropriate pool in the owning process.

content.text: Good idea — a system-model outline at that altitude belongs at the top of the messaging layer, above the detailed sections. Drafting it now (with one refinement to your fourth bullet: a remote free doesn't round-trip through the owning process — the freer pushes directly onto the pool's free-stack in shared memory, and the owning allocator simply sees the buffer on a later alloc):

input.old_string: ### Layer requirements
input.new_string:
### System model (overview)

The layer's rules at a glance; every statement is expanded
in the sections below.

- A process may have one or more pools; each pool has one
  owning allocator (a single thread allocates; any holder
  frees).
- Not all pools in a process need to be in shared memory —
  a private pool serves intra-process messaging.
- A message to be sent to another process must come from a
  pool in shared memory, and the receiver must have that
  pool mapped.
- Queues carry descriptors, not payloads: a uniform small
  `(pool id, buffer index)` regardless of message size; the
  payload stays at rest in its pool buffer.
- Any message travels over any queue, provided both
  endpoints have the message's pool registered.
- Each process keeps its own registry mapping pool ids to
  that process's view (mapping) of each pool.
- Ownership follows the descriptor: alloc → write → send
  the descriptor over a queue → the receiver owns it →
  read, forward over another queue, or free. A buffer is
  always in exactly one state with one permitted toucher.
- Any holder — any thread or process — may free; the free
  pushes the buffer directly onto its pool's free-stack in
  shared memory (no round-trip through the owning process),
  where the owning allocator finds it on a later alloc.
- Descriptors and free-stack links arriving through shared
  memory are untrusted: validated before use; corruption
  degrades to errors or lost buffers, never UB.
- 0.7.0 builds the in-process slice of this model;
  cross-process setup (mapping exchange, pool-id
  coordination) stays design.

### Layer requirements

content.text:
Added as `### System model (overview)` at the top of the messaging layer, before Layer requirements. The outline:

- A process may have one or more pools; each pool has one owning allocator (a single thread allocates; any holder frees).
- Not all pools in a process need to be in shared memory — a private pool serves intra-process messaging.
- A message to be sent to another process must come from a pool in shared memory, and the receiver must have that pool mapped.
- Queues carry descriptors, not payloads: a uniform small `(pool id, buffer index)` regardless of message size; the payload stays at rest in its pool buffer.
- Any message travels over any queue, provided both endpoints have the message's pool registered.
- Each process keeps its own registry mapping pool ids to that process's view (mapping) of each pool.
- Ownership follows the descriptor: alloc → write → send the descriptor over a queue → the receiver owns it → read, forward over another queue, or free. A buffer is always in exactly one state with one permitted toucher.
- Any holder — any thread or process — may free; the free pushes the buffer directly onto its pool's free-stack in shared memory (no round-trip through the owning process), where the owning allocator finds it on a later alloc.
- Descriptors and free-stack links arriving through shared memory are untrusted: validated before use; corruption degrades to errors or lost buffers, never UB.
- 0.7.0 builds the in-process slice of this model; cross-process setup (mapping exchange, pool-id coordination) stays design.

Your first, second, and third example bullets carried over nearly verbatim; the fourth became the "any holder may free" bullet with the direct-push refinement. ### System model (overview)

The layer's rules at a glance; each bullet links to the
section that expands it.

- A process may have one or more pools
  [details](#pool-topology-and-phasing).
  - Each pool has one owning allocator: a single thread
    allocates, any holder frees.
- Not all pools in a process need to be in shared memory
  [details](#layer-requirements).
  - A process-private pool serves intra-process messaging.
- A message to be sent to another process must come from a
  pool in shared memory
  [details](#provenance-and-descriptors).
  - The receiving process must have that pool mapped.
- Queues carry descriptors, not payloads
  [details](#provenance-and-descriptors).
  - A descriptor is a uniform small
    `(pool id, buffer index)` regardless of message size.
  - The payload stays at rest in its pool buffer.
- Any message travels over any queue
  [details](#layer-requirements).
  - Precondition: both endpoints have the message's pool
    registered.
- Each process keeps its own pool registry
  [details](#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
  - It maps pool ids to that process's view (mapping) of
    each pool.
- Ownership follows the descriptor
  [details](#usage-model-roles-and-buffer-lifecycle).
  - alloc → write → send the descriptor over a queue →
    the receiver owns it → read, forward over another
    queue, or free.
  - A buffer is always in exactly one state with one
    permitted toucher.
- Any holder — any thread or process — may free
  [details](#free-path-intrusive-lifo-free-stack).
  - The free pushes the buffer directly onto its pool's
    free-stack in shared memory — no round-trip through
    the owning process.
  - The owning allocator finds the buffer on a later
    alloc.
- Descriptors and free-stack links arriving through shared
  memory are untrusted
  [details](#provenance-and-descriptors).
  - Both are validated before use.
  - Corruption degrades to errors or lost buffers, never

All eight anchor targets verified against the actual headings (`#pool-topology-and-phasing`, `#layer-requirements`, `#provenance-and-descriptors`, `#descriptor-and-registry-design-070`, `#usage-model-roles-and-buffer-lifecycle`, `#free-path-intrusive-lifo-free-stack`, `#open-questions`).

The semicolon-joined clauses are gone: "descriptors not payloads" now has two sub-bullets (uniform `(pool id, buffer index)`; payload at rest), "any holder may free" has the direct-push and allocator-finds-it-later as separate sub-bullets, and the trust rule splits validation from the corruption-degradation guarantee. The one deliberate survivor is "a single thread allocates, any holder frees" inside the first sub-bullet — a tight pair where splitting felt like noise, per the convention's escape hatch.

content.text:
Worth doing, but selectively — here's my thinking.

You've spotted the real limitation: in GitHub markdown, headings are the *only* robustly linkable unit. A bullet has no anchor, so when a section covers five distinct topics as bullets, every inbound link lands at the section top and the reader scans. Promoting sub-topics to `####` headings fixes that — GitHub auto-generates anchors at every heading level, `#####` included.

The counterweight is fragmentation and anchor churn. The design doc's sections are mostly a screenful; landing at the top loses little there. And every heading you mint is an anchor contract — rename it and inbound links dangle (the checklist catches them, but it's churn). Turning every bullet into a `####` would make the doc read like an index rather than a narrative.

So the rule I'd adopt: **promote to a heading when two or more inbound links want different spots inside one section** — multiplicity of targets, not linkability in principle, is the signal. Applying that to the overview's current links:

- `#provenance-and-descriptors` — 3 links wanting different bullets (shared-memory precondition, descriptor shape, trust). This is the one genuine case: split its bullets into `####` subsections (Offsets only / Message provenance / Descriptors / Pool self-description / Pool-id resolution / Trust — or a slightly coarser grouping).
- `#descriptor-and-registry-design-070` — 3 links, but two of them ("registry", "0.7.0 slice") genuinely mean the whole section; I'd leave it intact.
- `#layer-requirements` — 2 links, section is ~15 lines; leave it.

`#####` I'd hold in reserve — nothing currently has two levels of finer detail, and deep nesting past `####` usually means the section wants to be its own `###` instead.

Want me to do the Provenance split now as a small docs commit before `-2`, fold it into `-2`'s commit when that lands, or just record the rule and apply it when a link actually annoys?

file.content:
### Provenance and descriptors

- **Offsets only, everywhere** — different per-process
  mappings mean no pointers in any shared structure; this
  also serves the single-address-space case unchanged.
- **Message provenance** — every buffer begins with a small
  header naming its pool `(pool id, offset)`; whoever holds a
  message last must free it to the right pool, so provenance
  travels with the message.
- **Descriptors** — a queue entry is a uniform small
  `(pool id, buffer index)` regardless of message size; the
  receiver resolves the pool id and learns geometry from the
  pool's own header (index over byte offset settled in
  [Descriptor and registry design](#descriptor-and-registry-design-070)).
- **Pool self-description** — each pool region starts with a
  header (magic, layout version, buffer size, count,
  alignment), attach-validated exactly like the ring header.
- **Pool-id resolution** — each process keeps a local registry
  mapping pool id to its own mapping of that pool's region.
  "Any message over any queue" carries the precondition that
  both endpoints have the message's pool mapped.
- **Trust** — offsets and indices arriving through shared
  memory are untrusted: bounds- and alignment-checked against
  the receiver's own view before use, same discipline as the
  ring's geometry snapshot.

input.old_string:
### Provenance and descriptors

- **Offsets only, everywhere** — different per-process
  mappings mean no pointers in any shared structure; this
  also serves the single-address-space case unchanged.
- **Message provenance** — every buffer begins with a small
  header naming its pool `(pool id, offset)`; whoever holds a
  message last must free it to the right pool, so provenance
  travels with the message.
- **Descriptors** — a queue entry is a uniform small
  `(pool id, buffer index)` regardless of message size; the
  receiver resolves the pool id and learns geometry from the
  pool's own header (index over byte offset settled in
  [Descriptor and registry design](#descriptor-and-registry-design-070)).
- **Pool self-description** — each pool region starts with a
  header (magic, layout version, buffer size, count,
  alignment), attach-validated exactly like the ring header.
- **Pool-id resolution** — each process keeps a local registry
  mapping pool id to its own mapping of that pool's region.
  "Any message over any queue" carries the precondition that
  both endpoints have the message's pool mapped.
- **Trust** — offsets and indices arriving through shared
  memory are untrusted: bounds- and alignment-checked against
  the receiver's own view before use, same discipline as the
  ring's geometry snapshot.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring**

Getting a pool buffer must not imply sending it, and the
ring alone fuses allocation, queue position, and
publication. Descriptors — small `(pool id, buffer offset)`
values sent through the existing SPSC ring — separate them,
so any pool message travels over any queue. This cycle
builds the in-process slice: the descriptor type, pool ids
via a per-process registry, and guard ↔ descriptor
conversion; cross-process setup (mapping exchange, pool-id
coordination) stays design
[details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).

- 0.7.0-0 chore: open descriptor queue cycle (done)
- 0.7.0-1 docs: settle descriptor design questions —
  descriptor shape (offset vs index, width), pool-id
  source, resolve-side validation, the unsafe ownership
  contract for guard ↔ descriptor (done)
- 0.7.0-2 feat: pool registry + descriptor resolve
- 0.7.0-3 test: descriptor protocol tests (round-trip,
  cross-thread free, hostile descriptors)
- 0.7.0-4 feat: demo descriptor flow ring + pool
- 0.7.0 feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring
  (close-out)

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

content.text: Now the implementation. Reading the pool internals I'll build on:

content.content:
1	//! Message pool: fixed-size buffers over a caller-provided
2	//! region, handed out through an intrusive LIFO free-stack —
3	//! the allocation half of the messaging layer, decoupling
4	//! "get a message" from "send it" (see the design doc's
5	//! "Messaging layer: pools and descriptor queues").
6	//!
7	//! - One `#[repr(C)]` [`PoolHeader`] spanning two cache lines
8	//!   (immutable geometry, free-stack head), followed by
9	//!   `buf_count` buffers of `buf_size` bytes each.
10	//! - The free-stack is intrusive with zero per-buffer
11	//!   overhead: a *free* buffer's first word holds the index
12	//!   of the next free buffer; an allocated buffer carries no
13	//!   header at all (pool-id provenance is deferred — see
14	//!   todo.md's In Progress block).
15	//! - One owning allocator pops ([`Pool::alloc`], `&mut self`
16	//!   as the in-process single-popper token); any holder
17	//!   frees ([`BufSlot::free`], the MPSC push side). The
18	//!   [`BufSlot`] guard does not borrow the pool, so any
19	//!   number of allocated buffers may be live at once —
20	//!   the decoupling the messaging layer exists for.
21	
22	use core::marker::PhantomData;
23	use core::mem::size_of;
24	use core::ops::{Deref, DerefMut};
25	use core::sync::atomic::{AtomicU32, Ordering};
26	use zerocopy::{FromBytes, IntoBytes, KnownLayout};
27	
28	use crate::{CACHE_LINE_SIZE, CacheAligned, Error, check_type};
29	
30	/// `alloc` failed: no free buffer (or a peer-corrupted
31	/// free-stack index — validation fails toward exhaustion,
32	/// never toward handing out memory the pool doesn't own).
33	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
34	pub struct Exhausted;
35	
36	/// Layout marker written by [`Pool::init`]; rejects foreign
37	/// (including ring) regions on attach.
38	const POOL_MAGIC: u32 = 0x5A43_5031; // "ZCP1"
39	
40	/// Bumped on any change to the pool region layout.
41	const POOL_LAYOUT_VERSION: u32 = 1;
42	
43	/// Free-stack terminator: no next free buffer.
44	///
45	/// - Also why `buf_count < u32::MAX`: every valid index must
46	///   be distinguishable from the sentinel.
47	const NIL: u32 = u32::MAX;
48	
49	/// Control block at offset 0 of a pool region — two cache
50	/// lines: cold geometry, then the contended free-stack head.
51	///
52	/// - line 0: geometry, written by [`Pool::init`] with `magic`
53	///   last (`Release`), read-only thereafter. Cold: per-op
54	///   paths use the handle's snapshot.
55	/// - line 1: `first_free_idx`, CAS-contended by every freer and
56	///   the allocator — sole owner of its line.
57	/// - Every field is atomic for the same reason as the ring
58	///   [`Header`](crate::Header): a peer may be mapped at any
59	///   time, and scribbles must be garbage values, never UB.
60	#[repr(C)]
61	pub struct PoolHeader {
62	    /// Layout marker ([`POOL_MAGIC`]); stored last by init
63	    /// (`Release`), loaded first by attach (`Acquire`).
64	    magic: AtomicU32,
65	    /// Pool layout version ([`POOL_LAYOUT_VERSION`]).
66	    layout_version: AtomicU32,
67	    /// Buffer size in bytes — a [`CACHE_LINE_SIZE`] multiple.
68	    buf_size: AtomicU32,
69	    /// Buffer count — nonzero, `< u32::MAX` ([`NIL`]).
70	    buf_count: AtomicU32,
71	    /// [`CACHE_LINE_SIZE`] this region was built with; attach
72	    /// validates it like the rest of the geometry.
73	    cache_line_size: AtomicU32,
74	    /// Free-stack head: index of the first free buffer, or
75	    /// [`NIL`] when the pool is exhausted.
76	    first_free_idx: CacheAligned<AtomicU32>,
77	}
78	
79	const _: () = assert!(size_of::<PoolHeader>() == 2 * CACHE_LINE_SIZE);
80	
81	/// A validated view over a pool region.
82	///
83	/// - Geometry is snapshotted out of the header at
84	///   init/attach: per-op paths never re-read fields a peer
85	///   could scribble on. Free-stack links live in shared
86	///   buffer memory and are validated at every pop instead.
87	pub struct Pool<'a> {
88	    /// The region's control block.
89	    header: &'a PoolHeader,
90	    /// Base of the buffer array
91	    /// (offset `size_of::<PoolHeader>()`).
92	    bufs: *mut u8,
93	    /// Snapshot of `header.buf_size`.
94	    buf_size: u32,
95	    /// Snapshot of `header.buf_count`.
96	    buf_count: u32,
97	    _region: PhantomData<&'a [u8]>,
98	}
99	
100	// SAFETY: the handle owns the allocator role; the shared
101	// state it touches (first_free_idx, next-links) is atomic,
102	// and buffer ownership is handed off with Release/Acquire
103	// ordering — same rationale as the ring endpoints.
104	unsafe impl Send for Pool<'_> {}
105	
106	impl<'a> Pool<'a> {

110	const _: () = assert!(size_of::<Header>() == 4 * CACHE_LINE_SIZE);
111	
112	/// Errors from region validation — [`Ring::init`] /
113	/// [`Ring::attach`] and [`Pool::init`] / [`Pool::attach`].
114	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
115	pub enum Error {
116	    /// Region is not [`CACHE_LINE_SIZE`]-aligned.
117	    Misaligned,
118	    /// Region is smaller than header + slots/buffers.
119	    TooSmall,
120	    /// Slot size is zero or not a [`CACHE_LINE_SIZE`] multiple.
121	    BadSlotSize,

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring**

Getting a pool buffer must not imply sending it, and the
ring alone fuses allocation, queue position, and
publication. Descriptors — small `(pool id, buffer offset)`
values sent through the existing SPSC ring — separate them,
so any pool message travels over any queue. This cycle
builds the in-process slice: the descriptor type, pool ids
via a per-process registry, and guard ↔ descriptor
conversion; cross-process setup (mapping exchange, pool-id
coordination) stays design
[details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).

- 0.7.0-0 chore: open descriptor queue cycle (done)
- 0.7.0-1 docs: settle descriptor design questions —
  descriptor shape (offset vs index, width), pool-id
  source, resolve-side validation, the unsafe ownership
  contract for guard ↔ descriptor (done)
- 0.7.0-2 feat: pool registry + descriptor resolve
  (current)
- 0.7.0-3 test: descriptor protocol tests (round-trip,
  cross-thread free, hostile descriptors)
- 0.7.0-4 feat: demo descriptor flow ring + pool
- 0.7.0 feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring
  (close-out)

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- feat: ring + pool demo binary [[8]]
- feat: demo alloc/free perf loops [[9]]
- feat: demo cpu-pinned placement variants [[10]]

content.text:
`-2` is complete and validated — ready for your review. What's in the working copy:

**New API (`src/registry.rs`, +`src/pool.rs` additions, exported from `lib.rs`):**

- `Desc { pool_id: u32, buf_idx: u32 }` — 8-byte POD with the zerocopy derives, so it rides the ring as an ordinary message. Fields are deliberately raw `u32`s and public: the wire form is untrusted by definition.
- `Pool::resolver() -> PoolResolver` — the derived non-allocating view (`Clone + Copy + Send + Sync`, with safety comments justifying each). It holds pub(crate) primitives only; the public surface goes through the registry.
- `PoolRegistry<'a, const N: usize>` — const-generic array, `const fn new()`, `register(resolver) -> PoolId`. `register` is `&mut self` (setup phase); `into_desc`/`resolve` are `&self` so one registry shares across threads by reference.
- `into_desc(pool_id, slot) -> Result<Desc, (BufSlot, RegistryError)>` — safe, O(1) header-identity check, guard handed back on error.
- `unsafe resolve::<T>(desc) -> Result<BufSlot<T>, RegistryError>` — all failures are `Err` (UnknownPoolId / BadIndex / BadType), with the ownership contract in its `# Safety` section exactly as the design doc states it. Uses a new non-panicking `type_fits` alongside `check_type` in `lib.rs`.

**Folded in per your direction:** the Provenance split — its six bullets are now `####` headings (Offsets only / Message provenance / Descriptors / Pool self-description / Pool-id resolution / Trust), the three System-model links retarget to `#pool-id-resolution`, `#descriptors`, `#trust`, and Message provenance gained a "(Deferred as of 0.7.0…)" note. Also fixed a stale pointer in `pool.rs`'s module doc that referenced "todo.md's In Progress block" for the deferral.

Plus the usual: version `0.7.0-2`, ladder entry flipped to done. Validation: fmt, clippy `-D warnings`, and all 3 test suites green (18 lib tests; new-code tests are the `-3` commit).

Say the word for the commit description.
