toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring**

Getting a pool buffer must not imply sending it, and the
ring alone fuses allocation, queue position, and
publication. Descriptors — small `(pool id, buffer offset)`
values sent through the existing SPSC ring — separate them,
so any pool message travels over any queue. This cycle
builds the in-process slice: the descriptor type, pool ids
via a per-process registry, and guard ↔ descriptor
conversion; cross-process setup (mapping exchange, pool-id
coordination) stays design
[details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).

- 0.7.0-0 chore: open descriptor queue cycle (done)
- 0.7.0-1 docs: settle descriptor design questions —
  descriptor shape (offset vs index, width), pool-id
  source, resolve-side validation, the unsafe ownership
  contract for guard ↔ descriptor (done)
- 0.7.0-2 feat: pool registry + descriptor resolve (done)
- 0.7.0-3 test: descriptor protocol tests (round-trip,
  cross-thread free, hostile descriptors)
- 0.7.0-4 feat: demo descriptor flow ring + pool
- 0.7.0 feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring
  (close-out)

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

lastPrompt: I'm thinking we should add tests here rather than -3 that way we have some assurances it's correct now, thoughts

content.text:
Tests are in and the ladder is updated. The six new tests in `src/registry.rs`:

- **`desc_round_trip`** — alloc → write → `into_desc` → `resolve` → read → free, then verifies the freed buffer is genuinely back (all 4 allocate again, 5th is `Exhausted`).
- **`into_desc_checks_pool_identity`** — mispaired id → `WrongPool` *and* the guard comes back usable (the no-leak contract), then succeeds with the right id.
- **`into_desc_unknown_pool_id`** — an id minted by a bigger registry has no entry in a smaller one → `UnknownPoolId`, guard returned.
- **`resolve_rejects_hostile_descs`** — invented descriptors: unknown pool id, out-of-range index, and a `T` too big for the pool's buffers — each the right `Err`, never a panic.
- **`register_full`** — capacity-1 registry rejects the second register.
- **`desc_over_ring_cross_thread`** — the composed protocol end-to-end: 10,000 messages, descriptors carrying pool-buffer ownership through the SPSC ring producer-thread → consumer-thread, 4 buffers recycling under pressure, sequence asserted on every message. This one is the real assurance — it exercises exactly the flow the `-3` demo (and iiac-perf) will run.

fmt clean, clippy `-D warnings` clean, 24/24 tests pass. `-2` now lands API + tests together, and the ladder records why. Ready for the commit description?

message.content: Is there a registery per process, If so and how does process X find process Y's registry or maybe there only one in shared memory and all processes use it? Maybe that's in our overview and I missed it?

content.text:
One registry **per process**, in private memory — and the key insight is that process X never needs to find process Y's registry at all. Here's the model:

The registry itself never crosses a process boundary; only **descriptors** do. When Y sends `Desc { pool_id: 3, buf_idx: 7 }`, X doesn't consult Y's registry — X's *own* registry must have, at slot 3, X's *own* mapping of that same pool. Each process holds a private table whose *values* differ (each process maps the shared pool region at a different local address) but whose *keys* — the pool ids — must agree. The id is the shared vocabulary; the registry is each process's private dictionary for it.

So the real cross-process requirement isn't "find the other registry," it's **id agreement**: every participating process must register the same pool under the same id. How ids get agreed is exactly the open question the design records (`Pool-id allocation`: coordinator-assigned vs derived from the shm object's identity). A registry living in shared memory would be an alternative, but it would defeat itself: the values are per-process pointers, which are meaningless in another process — the whole reason descriptors carry ids instead of pointers.

Your question does expose one honest gap: the current `register()` assigns ids sequentially by registration order, which only produces agreement when a single process assigns all ids (the in-process case we're building). Cross-process, registration order won't match, so the registry will need something like `register_at(id, resolver)` — register under an externally agreed id. That's a small future API addition, not a redesign, but it's worth writing down now.

The overview does say "Each process keeps its own pool registry / it maps pool ids to that process's view (mapping) of each pool" — so it's there, but the id-agreement precondition is implicit. Since `-2` isn't committed yet, let me make it explicit:

input.old_string:
- Each process keeps its own pool registry
  [details](#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
  - It maps pool ids to that process's view (mapping) of
    each pool.
input.new_string:

  [details](#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).

  - Registries are private and never shared; only
    descriptors cross process boundaries.
  - Pool ids are the shared vocabulary: every participating
    process must register a pool under the same id (the
    assignment scheme is an
    [open question](#open-questions)).

message.content: It's going to be an interesting problem. And this points out a problem in line 378 you point to open questions but which open question, I think open questions should be markdown sections so it is locatable. Thougts, no actions.

content.text:
Agreed on both counts — it's the same disease the Provenance split cured, and Open questions is if anything a *stronger* case for the treatment. Two thoughts:

**Why Open questions especially deserves headings.** Each open question is a natural unit of external reference — todo entries, chores sections, commit bodies, and now the System model all want to cite *a specific question*, not the pile. My `[open question](#open-questions)` link on line 378 is exactly the failure mode: the reader lands at the section top and has to hunt for "Pool-id allocation." So the trigger rule generalizes slightly: promote not just when two links want different spots, but when any link wants a target finer than what exists. `#### Pool-id allocation`, `#### Setup plane`, `#### Message header shape`.

**A pleasant property of the resolution lifecycle.** Open questions eventually migrate to `## Resolved questions`, which sounds like anchor churn — but GitHub anchors are derived from the heading *text*, not its position. Moving `#### Pool-id allocation` from Open to Resolved keeps `#pool-id-allocation` working in every inbound link, unchanged. So headings actually make the migration *safer* than bullets: today, resolving a question rewrites a bullet and silently strands any prose that described where to find it; with headings the link follows the question wherever it lives. The only rename hazard is retitling a question, which the pre-commit dangling-anchor check catches.

The one counterweight is the same as before — heading proliferation making the doc read like an index — but three questions with a paragraph each is well past the threshold where that concern loses; these are already reference targets in practice.

When you give the word I'd do the split and retarget line 378 to `#pool-id-allocation` — folded into `-2` like the Provenance split, since it's the same link-hygiene work and `-2` is still open.

(registry slot index, no unregister); cross-process
  remains open: coordinator-assigned versus derived from
  the shm object's identity.
- **Setup plane** — how a process discovers and maps a pool it
  hasn't seen: pre-arranged at init (embedded-friendly,
  allocation-free after startup) versus fd-passing over a
  Unix socket (Linux-friendly, dynamic); likely both, as
  profiles.
- **Message header shape** — 0.7.0 settled the near half:
  provenance travels in the descriptor, a type tag is
  payload-level, and the in-buffer header is deferred (see
  [Descriptor and registry design](#descriptor-and-registry-design-070)).
  Still open: whether a length (or anything else) joins a
  future in-buffer header, and its layout against the
  embedded next-link — the link is load-bearing for three
  states (free-stack, pending FIFO, future lists), so they
  must be laid out together when that header lands.

## Resolved questions

- **Cross-process trust** — resolved in 0.3.0-4: the
  all-atomic header plus the geometry snapshot mean a hostile
  peer cannot cause UB, only garbage messages (still valid
  `T`s — zerocopy guarantees representation, not sense) or
  spurious Full/Empty. `attach` remains `unsafe` for the
  mapping-liveness contract, not for peer trust.
- **`split()` once-guard** — direction chosen: an
  endpoint-claims word in the header (CAS to claim the
  producer / consumer role, second claimant gets an error),
  which also catches in-process double-attach; costs a
  layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`). Promoted to a
  `## Todo` entry in [todo.md](todo.md); the documented
  contract stands until then.

#### Pool-id allocation

In-process settled in 0.7.0 (registry slot index, no
unregister); cross-process remains open:
coordinator-assigned versus derived from the shm object's
identity.

#### Setup plane

How a process discovers and maps a pool it hasn't seen:
pre-arranged at init (embedded-friendly, allocation-free
after startup) versus fd-passing over a Unix socket
(Linux-friendly, dynamic); likely both, as profiles.

#### Message header shape

0.7.0 settled the near half: provenance travels in the
descriptor, a type tag is payload-level, and the in-buffer
header is deferred (see
[Descriptor and registry design](#descriptor-and-registry-design-070)).
Still open: whether a length (or anything else) joins a
future in-buffer header, and its layout against the
embedded next-link — the link is load-bearing for three
states (free-stack, pending FIFO, future lists), so they
must be laid out together when that header lands.

content.text:
**Proposed commit description:**

```
feat: pool registry + descriptor resolve

The descriptor layer's in-process slice: an 8-byte Desc
rides any queue as a POD message, a per-process
PoolRegistry turns guards into descriptors and back, with
protocol tests folded in so the API never lands untested.

- src/registry.rs (new)
  - Desc { pool_id, buf_idx }: POD wire form, raw u32
    fields by design
  - PoolId, RegistryError, PoolRegistry<N>: const-generic
    table, register -> PoolId, safe O(1) into_desc
    returning the guard on error, unsafe resolve with
    all-Err validation + ownership # Safety contract
  - tests: round-trip, pool-identity + unknown-id
    into_desc, hostile-desc resolve, registry full, 10k
    descs over the ring cross-thread with recycling
- src/pool.rs
  - PoolResolver: non-allocating Send+Sync view;
    Pool::resolver() derives it from the handle (no second
    region borrow); pub(crate) slot_from_idx mints guards
  - BufSlot: pub(crate) header_ptr/idx accessors
  - module doc: stale todo.md pointer -> design doc
- src/lib.rs: mod registry + exports; type_fits
  (non-panicking check_type for resolve)
- notes/ring-buffer-design.md
  - Provenance and descriptors: bullets promoted to ####
    headings (directly linkable); overview links retarget
  - Open questions: one #### per question; anchor survives
    resolution migration; links retarget
  - registry id-agreement notes: private registries, ids
    are the shared vocabulary, future register_at
- notes/todo.md: ladder -2 done (tests folded in), demo
  renumbered to -3
- Cargo.toml: version 0.7.0-2
- Cargo.lock: version
```

Approve and I'll `vc-x1 push main`.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring**

Getting a pool buffer must not imply sending it, and the
ring alone fuses allocation, queue position, and
publication. Descriptors — small `(pool id, buffer offset)`
values sent through the existing SPSC ring — separate them,
so any pool message travels over any queue. This cycle
builds the in-process slice: the descriptor type, pool ids
via a per-process registry, and guard ↔ descriptor
conversion; cross-process setup (mapping exchange, pool-id
coordination) stays design
[details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).

- 0.7.0-0 chore: open descriptor queue cycle (done)
- 0.7.0-1 docs: settle descriptor design questions —
  descriptor shape (offset vs index, width), pool-id
  source, resolve-side validation, the unsafe ownership
  contract for guard ↔ descriptor (done)
- 0.7.0-2 feat: pool registry + descriptor resolve —
  protocol tests folded in (round-trip, cross-thread free,
  hostile descriptors) so the API never lands untested
  (done)
- 0.7.0-3 feat: demo descriptor flow ring + pool
- 0.7.0 feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring
  (close-out)

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- docs: messaging layer design (pools + queues) [[6]]
- feat: message pool allocator [[7]]
- feat: ring + pool demo binary [[8]]
- feat: demo alloc/free perf loops [[9]]
- feat: demo cpu-pinned placement variants [[10]]

# References

[5]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-ring-buffer-user-line--blocking-contract
[6]: chores/chores-01.md#docs-messaging-layer-design-pools--queues

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring**

Getting a pool buffer must not imply sending it, and the
ring alone fuses allocation, queue position, and
publication. Descriptors — small `(pool id, buffer offset)`
values sent through the existing SPSC ring — separate them,
so any pool message travels over any queue. This cycle
builds the in-process slice: the descriptor type, pool ids
via a per-process registry, and guard ↔ descriptor
conversion; cross-process setup (mapping exchange, pool-id
coordination) stays design
[details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).

- 0.7.0-0 chore: open descriptor queue cycle (done)
- 0.7.0-1 docs: settle descriptor design questions —
  descriptor shape (offset vs index, width), pool-id
  source, resolve-side validation, the unsafe ownership
  contract for guard ↔ descriptor (done)
- 0.7.0-2 feat: pool registry + descriptor resolve —
  protocol tests folded in (round-trip, cross-thread free,
  hostile descriptors) so the API never lands untested
  (done)
- 0.7.0-3 feat: demo descriptor flow ring + pool (current)
- 0.7.0 feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring
  (close-out)

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

```

What the numbers say (single runs, usual caveat): the composed 1t at 11.8 ns is almost exactly ring (2.7) + pool alloc/free (9.8) — the descriptor layer itself adds nearly nothing. Same-core composed beats the std-channel version (44.5 vs 59.4 ns), the apples-to-apples line iiac-perf will want. Diff-cores composed (317.7) came in *above* std_mpsc (284.8) this run — plausibly real (the composed flow ping-pongs ring lines *and* free-stack *and* buffer lines across L3 domains) but within the swing we've seen between runs; the mean/stdev work would settle it.

The changes: two new demo functions (`spsc_ring_one_pool_msg_1t` pinned to core 0, `_2t` with the full pin ladder) running alloc → `into_desc` → `Desc` through the ring → `resolve` → assert → free; module doc updated (composed form "runs between them" instead of "is the next cycle"); the stale footer line removed; overview gained the "as built, everything is single-process" sub-bullet you asked for; version `0.7.0-3`, ladder marked done. Validation: fmt, clippy `-D warnings`, 24/24 tests, install (binary reports `0.7.0-3`).

Ready for the commit description?

message.content: We should be able to get a desc for a pool outside the loop and just populate the buf and I suggest renaming buf to buf_slot?

content.text:
Two parts to this — the rename is straightforward, but "get a desc outside the loop" runs into the ownership design in an instructive way. Let me explain what I'll do:

The guard (`BufSlot`) and the descriptor are the *two exclusive forms of ownership* — `into_desc` consumes the guard because holding both would let the sender write while the receiver reads. So "populate the buf" and "already have the desc" can't coexist; the conversion *is* the send-side handoff. What we **can** hoist in the 1t flow is the alloc/free: alloc once outside the timed loop, and inside just populate → `into_desc` → ring → `resolve` → assert, with the resolved guard carrying into the next iteration and one `free()` after the loop. That isolates pure messaging cost (conversion + ring transfer) from the pool cycle. The 2t flow can't hoist at all: ownership genuinely leaves the producer each message, so the producer's next buffer must come from the free-stack — alloc/free per message *is* that flow, not overhead. Implementing:

content.text:
Both changes are in, validated, and the hoist paid off visibly:

- **`spsc_ring_one_pool_msg_1t`** now allocs one message before the timed loop; each iteration populates it, `into_desc` → ring → `resolve` brings the guard back for the next iteration, and the single `free()` lands after the loop. The per-iteration conversion stays — the guard and descriptor are the two exclusive ownership forms, so converting *is* the handoff and can't hoist. The doc comment records both that and why the 2t variant keeps alloc/free per message (ownership genuinely leaves the producer there). Effect: 1t composed dropped from ~11.9 to **7.7 ns/msg** — pure messaging cost (ring 2.6 + conversion/resolve ~5), with the pool cycle now cleanly separated into `pool_alloc_free_1t`'s line.
- **`buf` → `buf_slot`** on every `BufSlot`-typed local (composed 2t, both std_mpsc functions, `pool_alloc_free_1t`). I left `global_alloc_free_1t`'s local as `buf` since it's a `Box<Msg>`, not a `BufSlot` — renaming it would claim a type it isn't.

message.content: This is a lot of code to get a message and send it, we need to make it two or three lines, thoughts no actions

content.text:
Agreed — and the fix is a layer, not sugar. Thoughts:

**Target shape.** What the demo loop wants to be:

```rust
let mut msg = tx.loan_spin::<Msg>();   // alloc from the sender's pool
msg.seq = i;
tx.send_spin(msg);                     // into_desc + reserve + write + commit
```
```rust
let msg = rx.recv_spin::<Msg>();       // reserve + read desc + release + resolve
assert_eq!(msg.seq, i);
msg.free();
```

**The mechanism: paired endpoints, not helper functions.** A `DescSender` owning `(Producer, PoolId, &PoolRegistry — or the Pool itself)` and a `DescReceiver` owning `(Consumer, &PoolRegistry)`. The composite operations collapse the ceremony, but the deep win is safety, not line count: today every call site carries the `unsafe resolve` and its exactly-once contract. If descriptors only enter the ring through the paired sender's `send` — which consumed a guard to produce each one — then the receiver's `recv` satisfies resolve's contract *by construction*, and `recv` can be a **safe** function with the `unsafe` audited once, inside the crate, instead of at every user call site. The `map_err(|(_, e)| e)` noise disappears too, because users never touch a `Desc` at all — it becomes an implementation detail of the pair.

**Blocking stays layered.** The primitives stay non-blocking (`send` → `Err((msg, Full))` handing the guard back so nothing leaks, `recv` → `Err(Empty)`, `loan` → `Err(Exhausted)`), preserving the embedded floor; `_spin` variants are three-line conveniences on top. The futex/eventfd blocking layer from Ideas would later wrap these same endpoints.

**Owning alloc on the sender is the natural home.** `tx.loan()` — iceoryx2's loan/send shape exactly, which the design doc already told us to steal from. It also carries the single-popper token naturally: the sender is `&mut`-exclusive, so "one owning allocator" becomes structural rather than documented.

**One design question to settle when we do it:** does the sender own the `Pool` (simple, but locks one pool per sender) or take `(PoolId, BufSlot)` per send (any registered pool over one queue — the "any message over any queue" promise)? Probably: own a default pool for `loan`, but accept any guard in `send` via the registry.

**Where it goes:** not this commit — `-3` is the demo, and this is a real API cycle ("feat: descriptor queue endpoints", or `loan`/`send`/`recv` naming to taste). I'd add it as a `## Todo` entry at rank #1 when we close 0.7.0 — arguably it should even precede the iiac-perf work, since iiac-perf would otherwise inline all this ceremony too.

Want me to add the todo entry as part of `-3`'s notes changes, or hold everything for the close-out?

message.content: I wonder we make an intermediate later where what to do on Empty/Full is a closure, so something like: `buf_slot =  pool.get_slot<Msg>(||spin_loop)` thoughts?
I wonder we we might use a closure on Empty/Full,  something like: `buf_slot =  pool.get_slot<Msg>(||spin_loop)` thoughts?
I wonder if we might use a closure for Empty/Full handling,  something like: `buf_slot =  pool.get_slot<Msg>(||spin_loop)` thoughts?

content.text:
Yes — that's the right generalization, with one refinement. Thoughts:

**A closure turns the wait loop into an injected policy.** `_spin` variants hard-code one policy; a closure parameter makes the operation generic over *how to wait* — spin, `yield_now`, sleep-with-backoff, whatever — so we don't grow a method per policy per operation. The demo's nine-line loop becomes exactly your sketch, and `_spin` (if we keep it at all) is just the closure pre-filled.

**The refinement: let the closure vote on giving up.** A `FnMut()` that can only spin loops forever — fine for a demo, wrong for real callers who need timeouts. Make it `FnMut(u32) -> bool` (attempt count in, "keep waiting" out):

```rust
pub fn alloc_with<T>(&mut self, mut wait: impl FnMut(u32) -> bool)
    -> Result<BufSlot<'a, T>, Exhausted>
```

Then the whole policy space falls out of one signature: pure spin `|_| { spin_loop(); true }`, bounded `|n| n < 10_000`, progressive backoff `|n| { if n < 100 { spin_loop() } else { yield_now() }; true }`, and a deadline via a captured `Instant`. `Err` comes back only when the policy quits, so the non-blocking contract survives — the primitive `alloc`/`reserve_slot` stay exactly as they are, and `_with` is a layer over them. A few canned policies as plain `fn`s (`policy::spin`, `policy::spin_then_yield(n)`) would cover the common cases without any trait machinery.

**It applies uniformly** to all three wait points — `alloc`/`Exhausted`, producer `reserve_slot`/`Full`, consumer `reserve_slot`/`Empty` — and composes perfectly with the endpoint layer from the last discussion: `loan_with`, `send_with`, `recv_with` all take the same policy shape. That uniformity is the argument for doing it as one design rather than a `get_slot` one-off on `Pool`.

**One honest limitation to record:** a closure policy covers *polling* strategies only. True blocking (futex, eventfd, async wakers) needs the other endpoint to issue a wake — a protocol over the header's user line, not something a wait closure can do alone. So this doesn't replace the blocking-layer Idea; it's the tier between "raw non-blocking" and "real blocking," and probably eliminates 90% of the need for the latter.

Both this and the endpoints feel like one coherent follow-up cycle: endpoints give the 3-line shape, wait policies give the loop-free waits. I'd write them as a single `## Todo` entry ("descriptor queue endpoints + wait policies") at close-out. Sound right?

message.content: So we have more than one "closure" Type?

content.text:
Depends which sense of "type" — the answer is "one signature, many machine types, our choice of how to accept them":

**One signature.** All three wait points (alloc/`Exhausted`, producer/`Full`, consumer/`Empty`) take the same shape: `FnMut(u32) -> bool`. The policy never needs to know *what* it's waiting for — the operation retries its own condition; the closure just decides whether/how to keep waiting. So conceptually there's exactly one "wait policy" contract in the design.

**Many concrete types.** In Rust every closure *expression* has its own unique, unnameable type — even two textually identical `|n| n < 100`s are distinct types. `FnMut(u32) -> bool` is not a type; it's a trait bound that all of them satisfy.

**Three ways the API can accept that**, and this is the real design choice:

- `impl FnMut(u32) -> bool` (generic) — monomorphized: each distinct closure stamps out its own specialized copy of `alloc_with`, fully inlined, zero overhead. The spin policy compiles to literally the loop we write by hand today. Cost: one copy of the function per distinct policy in the binary.
- `&mut dyn FnMut(u32) -> bool` (trait object) — one shared copy of the function, closure called through an indirect call. A hair slower per retry, but retries are the slow path anyway. This is the code-size-friendly option if an embedded target ever cares.
- `fn(u32) -> bool` (plain function pointer) — no captures allowed, so no deadline-carrying policies, but the canned ones (`policy::spin`) are just ordinary `fn`s and coerce to this.

I'd take `impl FnMut(u32) -> bool` in the public API — it's the idiomatic zero-cost choice, and it accepts all three kinds above (plain `fn`s and capturing closures alike). If binary bloat ever shows up on an embedded build, an internal `dyn` core with a thin generic wrapper gets one copy without changing the API. So: one policy concept, one bound, and monomorphization details stay invisible to callers.

message.content: I was thinking multiple signatures, in the end users can compose to whatever they need, the simplest is just their own "get<Msg>()" and no closure, but we should supply common ones we use as a model.

content.text:
Right — that reframes what the shipped helpers are *for*: not coverage of the policy space, but worked examples proving the composition works. The tiers:

1. **Primitive (exists, unchanged):** `alloc` / `reserve_slot` → immediate `Err(Exhausted / Full / Empty)`. The only tier that knows the protocol; everything above is composition, so nothing above can break an invariant.
2. **The hook:** `alloc_with(policy)` / endpoint `loan_with` / `send_with` / `recv_with` — the one seam where a wait policy plugs in.
3. **Shipped models:** the handful we ourselves use in the demo and tests — probably just `_spin` (`= _with(|_| { spin_loop(); true })`) and maybe a bounded variant. Each is a two-line wrapper whose body *is* the documentation: "here's how you build yours."
4. **User compositions:** their own `get::<Msg>()` — no closure in *their* signature because the policy is baked into their wrapper, exactly as domain code wants it. An app writes `fn get(&mut self) -> Msg` once, with its own timeout/backoff/panic-on-starvation choice, and the rest of the app never sees a wait loop or a closure again.

The discipline that keeps it honest: the crate ships mechanisms plus only the policies it dogfoods — anything we ship must be used by our own demo/tests, or it's speculative API. And tier 4 costing the user two lines is the proof the seam is right; if their `get` needs more than that, the hook is wrong.

This all folds into the close-out todo entry — "descriptor queue endpoints + wait-policy hook, shipped policies as models" — and the demo itself becomes the first tier-4 consumer when that cycle lands. For *this* commit I'd leave the demo's explicit loops as-is; they're the honest picture of today's API, and their verbosity is now a recorded motivation for the next cycle. Still holding for your word on the `-3` description.

message.content: And in iiac-perf or a variant specifically for zc-ring-x1, the performance can be measured.

content.text:
Exactly — that's the missing piece that makes the whole layering defensible. The demo stays an eyeball smoke-baseline (single runs, no stats — as you flagged earlier), and iiac-perf's calibrated harness answers the questions the demo can't:

- **Is the abstraction actually free?** The claim that `send`/`recv` endpoints and a monomorphized wait closure compile to the same code as today's hand-rolled loops is verifiable: benchmark the tier-3 wrapper against the inline loop — the delta should be zero within noise. If it isn't, the seam is wrong and we find out *before* users build on it.
- **The real comparison with statistics:** composed descriptor flow vs `std::mpsc` vs crossbeam etc., mean/stdev/p99 across placements — the `zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirrors of `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t` the Ideas entry already sketches, now with the composed `zcring-pool-2t` joining them. The tail-latency claim in the Ideas notes (pool beats malloc on p99 because no arena locks, no brk/mmap) is also only answerable there.
- **Policy costs:** spin vs spin-then-yield vs bounded — measurable per policy since each is just a closure handed to the same hook.

Whether it lives in iiac-perf proper or a zc-ring-specific variant can be decided when we get there — iiac-perf proper seems right since cross-library comparison is its whole point, and the endpoints will make the harness code as short there as in the demo.

So the sequencing after 0.7.0 closes: endpoints + wait-policy cycle first (gives the 3-line API), then the iiac-perf integration measures it. I'll capture both in the close-out's todo entries. Ready to show the `-3` commit description whenever you are.

message.content: sg, I like to suggest the next to do is create a spin "closure" plus a _spin variants and then test all three in iiac_perf
sg, I like to suggest the #1 Todo be that we create a spin "closure" plus a _spin variants and then test all three (raw, closure, _spin) in iiac_perf.

content.text:
Noted — that ordering is smart: the wait-policy hook + `_spin` + the three-way iiac-perf comparison (raw loop vs `_with(closure)` vs `_spin`) *validates the seam empirically before* the endpoints cycle builds on it. I'll write that as Todo #1 at close-out, with endpoints as #2 depending on its result.

The composed flow joins the demo: pool messages travel as
descriptors through the SPSC ring (alloc -> into_desc ->
ring -> resolve -> free), with the same placement ladder as
the raw ring; output regrouped so like compares with like.

- src/bin/zc-ring-x1-demo.rs
  - spsc_ring_one_pool_msg_1t: one message allocated
    outside the timed loop, per-iteration populate ->
    into_desc -> ring -> resolve; isolates messaging cost
    from the pool cycle (doc explains why the conversion
    itself cannot hoist)
  - spsc_ring_one_pool_msg_2t(pin): producer allocs +
    converts, consumer resolves + frees, full pin ladder
  - std_mpsc_one_pool_msg_1t: single-thread std-channel
    counterpart so each family has a 1t baseline
  - main regrouped by placement: alloc/free baselines
    first, then 1t / 2t-unpinned / same-core / diff-cores
    blocks (blank-line separated); one skip line per block
  - BufSlot locals renamed buf -> buf_slot; stale footer
    line removed; module doc: composed form is present
    tense
- notes/ring-buffer-design.md: overview records that
  as-built everything is single-process
- notes/todo.md: ladder -3 marked done
- Cargo.toml: version 0.7.0-3
- Cargo.lock: version
```

Approve and I'll `vc-x1 push main`.

content.content:
## feat: message pool allocator

Commits: [[15]],[[16]],[[17]],[[18]],[[19]],[[20]]

Todo #1 (decouple "get a message" from "send it"): the ring's
reserve_slot fuses allocation, queue position, and a
publication window, so getting a buffer implied sending it
now. The pool is the allocation half of the messaging layer —
simplicity-first, to hone the API and give iiac-perf a
baseline.

- Region shape mirrors the ring: all-atomic two-line
  PoolHeader (cold geometry / contended `first_free_idx`),
  magic "ZCP1", magic-last init/attach handshake, geometry
  snapshots, hostile-peer posture (validated pops fail toward
  Exhausted, never out of bounds).
- Intrusive LIFO free-stack with zero per-buffer overhead: a
  free buffer's first word is its next-link; allocated
  buffers carry no header. Pool-id provenance deferred to the
  descriptor-queue cycle (pool layout_version bump).
- Ownership decoupling: `BufSlot` borrows the region
  lifetime, not the pool handle — many live guards at once;
  `alloc` takes `&mut self` as the single-popper token (no
  ABA); `free(self)` is the MPSC push, callable wherever the
  guard moved; drop-without-free leaks (RAII alternative
  recorded in Ideas).
- Usage model written before the tests (README: roles, buffer
  lifecycle, what "send" means pre-descriptor-queues) and the
  tests pin it: 11 pool tests incl. cross-magic attach
  rejection, corruption-repair, type edges (mixed sizes,
  align(32), too-big panics, ZST), threaded allocator/freer
  split; all under Miri.
- Naming honed in review: `cache_line_size` (verb-free),
  `first_free_idx`, `next_buf_idx`, `CACHE_LINE_SIZE` /
  `TEST_CACHE_LINE_SIZE`.

Ladder (all shipped, Keep separate):

- 0.6.0-0 chore: open message pool cycle
- 0.6.0-1 feat: message pool layout + init/attach
- 0.6.0-2 feat: message pool alloc/free
- 0.6.0-3 docs: message pool usage model
- 0.6.0-4 test: message pool protocol tests
- 0.6.0 feat: message pool allocator (close-out)

## feat: ring + pool demo binary

content.content:
140	
141	## Usage model: roles and buffer lifecycle
142	
143	Who may do what, per object and per buffer state. The tests
144	exercise exactly what this model permits; anything outside it
145	is a contract violation even where the compiler cannot reject
146	it.
147	
148	**Roles.** Roles are per *object*, and one thread may hold
149	several roles across objects (a typical sender is one pool's
150	allocator and one ring's producer):
151	
152	- **Ring** — exactly one producer and one consumer, ever
153	  (SPSC): in-process, `split()` hands out each endpoint
154	  once; cross-process, one producing and one consuming
155	  process by contract. An endpoint reserves at most one
156	  slot at a time (guard holds the endpoint borrow).
157	- **Pool** — exactly one *allocator* at a time (the
158	  single-popper contract on `Pool::attach`; in-process,
159	  `alloc` takes `&mut self`), and **any number of freers**:
160	  whoever holds a `BufSlot` may free it, from any thread or
161	  process attached to the pool.
162	
163	**Buffer lifecycle.** A pool buffer is always in exactly one
164	state, with one party allowed to touch it:
165	
166	- **free** — on the free-stack; the pool protocol owns it.
167	  Nobody reads or writes it except through alloc/free
168	  machinery (its first word is the next-link).
169	- **allocated** — popped by `alloc`, owned by the `BufSlot`
170	  holder: exclusive read/write through the guard, held as
171	  long as desired, moved between threads freely (`Send`).
172	  No other party — including the pool — may touch the
173	  bytes.
174	- **in-flight** — *placeholder, vacant this cycle*: after a
175	  send over a descriptor queue the sender must no longer
176	  touch the buffer and the receiver owns it on receipt.
177	  Until descriptor queues exist there is no in-flight
178	  state.
179	- **freed** — `BufSlot::free` pushes it back; ownership
180	  returns to the pool protocol the instant the CAS lands.
181	  Use-after-free of the guard is unrepresentable (`free`
182	  consumes it); dropping without `free` leaks the buffer.
183	
184	**What "send" means this cycle.** Transferring a message =
185	moving its `BufSlot` (an ordinary Rust move; `Send` lets it
186	cross threads). The ring and the pool do not yet compose:
187	a `BufSlot` cannot travel through a ring slot, and no API
188	reconstructs buffer access from a bare index — both arrive
189	with the descriptor-queue cycle. Ring messages this cycle
190	are by-value `T`s in slots, as before.
191	
192	**Where this is heading.** The ring alone is a building
193	block; the payoff is the two composed — the ring carrying
194	tiny descriptors while payloads stay put in pool buffers.
195	Illustrative only (this API does not exist yet; names will
196	change):
197	
198	```rust,ignore
199	// Sender: pool allocator + ring producer.
200	let mut msg = pool.alloc::<Msg>()?; // get a message
201	msg.val = 42;                       // fill it in place
202	producer.send(msg)?;                // consumes the BufSlot;
203	                                    // only its index travels
204	                                    // through the ring
205	
206	// Receiver: ring consumer + freer (other thread or process).
207	let desc = consumer.reserve_slot::<Desc>()?;
208	let msg = pool.resolve::<Msg>(&desc)?; // zero-copy view
209	//                  ... read msg ...
210	msg.free();     // buffer back to its pool
211	desc.release(); // ring slot free for the next descriptor
212	```
213	
214	One allocation's bytes are written once and never copied —
215	not by send, not by receive, not across the process
216	boundary.
217	
218	**Trust.** Unchanged from the ring: every word in shared
219	memory is untrusted input. The pool validates the head and
220	next-link at every pop and fails toward `Exhausted`; a
221	hostile peer can degrade service (garbage messages, lost
222	buffers, spurious exhaustion), never cause UB on this side.
223	
224	## Testing
225	
226	- `cargo test` — the full suite, including a threaded stress
227	  test and a u32-index-wrap test.
228	- `cargo run --example readme` — the Overview example above
229	  (committed as [examples/readme.rs](examples/readme.rs) so
230	  `cargo clippy --all-targets` keeps it compiling against the
231	  real API).
232	- `cargo run --example pool_readme` — the Message pool
233	  example above
234	  ([examples/pool_readme.rs](examples/pool_readme.rs), same
235	  convention).
236	- `cargo run --release` — the demo binary
237	  ([src/bin/zc-ring-x1-demo.rs](src/bin/zc-ring-x1-demo.rs)):
238	  a four-line throughput scoreboard (msgs/sec and ns/msg) —
239	  the ring moving typed messages in place across threads,
240	  the pool's allocator/freer role split (guards crossing a
241	  std channel), the pool's raw single-thread alloc/free,
242	  and the same loop through the global allocator for
243	  comparison. Eyeball numbers, not a benchmark (calibrated
244	  measurement lives in iiac-perf). Installable:
245	  `cargo install --path . --locked`, then `zc-ring-x1-demo`;
246	  `-V` prints the version-of-record so you know which build
247	  you are testing. An example run:
248	
249	  ```text
250	  $ zc-ring-x1-demo
251	  zc-ring-x1 0.6.2
252	  demo: 1,000,000 messages each, depth 64
253	  ring (SPSC, in-place):             18,723,550 msgs/sec     53.4 ns/msg
254	  pool (alloc here -> free there):    2,993,315 msgs/sec    334.1 ns/msg
255	  pool (alloc/free, 1 thread):      100,962,781 msgs/sec      9.9 ns/msg
256	  global (Box::new/drop, 1 thread):  107,037,570 msgs/sec      9.3 ns/msg
257	  (composed descriptor flow arrives with the descriptor-queue cycle)
258	  ```
259	- `cargo +nightly miri test` — the full suite under
260	  [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri), which checks the
261	  `unsafe` code against the memory model; it has caught two
262	  real Stacked Borrows bugs here (an init retag in 0.3.0-4, a
263	  guard aliasing race in 0.3.0-6 — the latter only visible
264	  with the threaded test included). One-time setup:
265	  `rustup component add --toolchain nightly miri`. The
266	  threaded stress test runs a reduced message count under
267	  Miri (interpreted spin loops are slow).
268	
269	## Releasing

## Usage model: roles and buffer lifecycle

Who may do what, per object and per buffer state. The tests
exercise exactly what this model permits; anything outside it
is a contract violation even where the compiler cannot reject
it.

**Roles.** Roles are per *object*, and one thread may hold
several roles across objects (a typical sender is one pool's
allocator and one ring's producer):

- **Ring** — exactly one producer and one consumer, ever
  (SPSC): in-process, `split()` hands out each endpoint
  once; cross-process, one producing and one consuming
  process by contract. An endpoint reserves at most one
  slot at a time (guard holds the endpoint borrow).
- **Pool** — exactly one *allocator* at a time (the
  single-popper contract on `Pool::attach`; in-process,
  `alloc` takes `&mut self`), and **any number of freers**:
  whoever holds a `BufSlot` may free it, from any thread or
  process attached to the pool.

**Buffer lifecycle.** A pool buffer is always in exactly one
state, with one party allowed to touch it:

- **free** — on the free-stack; the pool protocol owns it.
  Nobody reads or writes it except through alloc/free
  machinery (its first word is the next-link).
- **allocated** — popped by `alloc`, owned by the `BufSlot`
  holder: exclusive read/write through the guard, held as
  long as desired, moved between threads freely (`Send`).
  No other party — including the pool — may touch the
  bytes.
- **in-flight** — *placeholder, vacant this cycle*: after a
  send over a descriptor queue the sender must no longer
  touch the buffer and the receiver owns it on receipt.
  Until descriptor queues exist there is no in-flight
  state.
- **freed** — `BufSlot::free` pushes it back; ownership
  returns to the pool protocol the instant the CAS lands.
  Use-after-free of the guard is unrepresentable (`free`
  consumes it); dropping without `free` leaks the buffer.

**What "send" means this cycle.** Transferring a message =
moving its `BufSlot` (an ordinary Rust move; `Send` lets it
cross threads). The ring and the pool do not yet compose:
a `BufSlot` cannot travel through a ring slot, and no API
reconstructs buffer access from a bare index — both arrive
with the descriptor-queue cycle. Ring messages this cycle
are by-value `T`s in slots, as before.

**Where this is heading.** The ring alone is a building
block; the payoff is the two composed — the ring carrying
tiny descriptors while payloads stay put in pool buffers.
Illustrative only (this API does not exist yet; names will
change):

```rust,ignore
// Sender: pool allocator + ring producer.
let mut msg = pool.alloc::<Msg>()?; // get a message
msg.val = 42;                       // fill it in place
producer.send(msg)?;                // consumes the BufSlot;
                                    // only its index travels
                                    // through the ring

// Receiver: ring consumer + freer (other thread or process).
let desc = consumer.reserve_slot::<Desc>()?;
let msg = pool.resolve::<Msg>(&desc)?; // zero-copy view
//                  ... read msg ...
msg.free();     // buffer back to its pool
desc.release(); // ring slot free for the next descriptor
```

One allocation's bytes are written once and never copied —
not by send, not by receive, not across the process
boundary.

**Trust.** Unchanged from the ring: every word in shared
memory is untrusted input. The pool validates the head and
next-link at every pop and fails toward `Exhausted`; a
hostile peer can degrade service (garbage messages, lost
buffers, spurious exhaustion), never cause UB on this side.

## Testing

- `cargo test` — the full suite, including a threaded stress
  test and a u32-index-wrap test.
- `cargo run --example readme` — the Overview example above
  (committed as [examples/readme.rs](examples/readme.rs) so
  `cargo clippy --all-targets` keeps it compiling against the
  real API).
- `cargo run --example pool_readme` — the Message pool
  example above
  ([examples/pool_readme.rs](examples/pool_readme.rs), same
  convention).
- `cargo run --release` — the demo binary
  ([src/bin/zc-ring-x1-demo.rs](src/bin/zc-ring-x1-demo.rs)):
  a four-line throughput scoreboard (msgs/sec and ns/msg) —
  the ring moving typed messages in place across threads,
  the pool's allocator/freer role split (guards crossing a
  std channel), the pool's raw single-thread alloc/free,
  and the same loop through the global allocator for
  comparison. Eyeball numbers, not a benchmark (calibrated
  measurement lives in iiac-perf). Installable:
  `cargo install --path . --locked`, then `zc-ring-x1-demo`;
  `-V` prints the version-of-record so you know which build
  you are testing. An example run:

  ```text
  $ zc-ring-x1-demo
  zc-ring-x1 0.6.2
  demo: 1,000,000 messages each, depth 64
  ring (SPSC, in-place):             18,723,550 msgs/sec     53.4 ns/msg
  pool (alloc here -> free there):    2,993,315 msgs/sec    334.1 ns/msg
  pool (alloc/free, 1 thread):      100,962,781 msgs/sec      9.9 ns/msg
  global (Box::new/drop, 1 thread):  107,037,570 msgs/sec      9.3 ns/msg
  (composed descriptor flow arrives with the descriptor-queue cycle)
  ```
- `cargo +nightly miri test` — the full suite under
  [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri), which checks the
  `unsafe` code against the memory model; it has caught two
  real Stacked Borrows bugs here (an init retag in 0.3.0-4, a
  guard aliasing race in 0.3.0-6 — the latter only visible
  with the threaded test included). One-time setup:
  `rustup component add --toolchain nightly miri`. The
  threaded stress test runs a reduced message count under
  Miri (interpreted spin loops are slow).

## Releasing

name = "zc-ring-x1"

edition = "2024"
rust-version = "1.96"

## In Progress

**feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring**

Getting a pool buffer must not imply sending it, and the
ring alone fuses allocation, queue position, and
publication. Descriptors — small `(pool id, buffer offset)`
values sent through the existing SPSC ring — separate them,
so any pool message travels over any queue. This cycle
builds the in-process slice: the descriptor type, pool ids
via a per-process registry, and guard ↔ descriptor
conversion; cross-process setup (mapping exchange, pool-id
coordination) stays design
[details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).

- 0.7.0-0 chore: open descriptor queue cycle (done)
- 0.7.0-1 docs: settle descriptor design questions —
  descriptor shape (offset vs index, width), pool-id
  source, resolve-side validation, the unsafe ownership
  contract for guard ↔ descriptor (done)
- 0.7.0-2 feat: pool registry + descriptor resolve —
  protocol tests folded in (round-trip, cross-thread free,
  hostile descriptors) so the API never lands untested
  (done)
- 0.7.0-3 feat: demo descriptor flow ring + pool (done)
- 0.7.0 feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring
  (close-out)

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

**feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring**

Getting a pool buffer must not imply sending it, and the
ring alone fuses allocation, queue position, and
publication. Descriptors — small `(pool id, buffer offset)`
values sent through the existing SPSC ring — separate them,
so any pool message travels over any queue. This cycle
builds the in-process slice: the descriptor type, pool ids
via a per-process registry, and guard ↔ descriptor
conversion; cross-process setup (mapping exchange, pool-id
coordination) stays design
[details](ring-buffer-design.md#messaging-layer-pools-and-descriptor-queues).

- 0.7.0-0 chore: open descriptor queue cycle (done)
- 0.7.0-1 docs: settle descriptor design questions —
  descriptor shape (offset vs index, width), pool-id
  source, resolve-side validation, the unsafe ownership
  contract for guard ↔ descriptor (done)
- 0.7.0-2 feat: pool registry + descriptor resolve —
  protocol tests folded in (round-trip, cross-thread free,
  hostile descriptors) so the API never lands untested
  (done)
- 0.7.0-3 feat: demo descriptor flow ring + pool (done)
- 0.7.0 feat: descriptor queues over the SPSC ring
  (close-out)

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

- feat: ring buffer user line + blocking contract [[5]]
- docs: messaging layer design (pools + queues) [[6]]
- feat: message pool allocator [[7]]
- feat: ring + pool demo binary [[8]]
- feat: demo alloc/free perf loops [[9]]
- feat: demo cpu-pinned placement variants [[10]]

# References

[5]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-ring-buffer-user-line--blocking-contract
[6]: chores/chores-01.md#docs-messaging-layer-design-pools--queues
[7]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-message-pool-allocator
[8]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-ring--pool-demo-binary
[9]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-demo-allocfree-perf-loops
[10]: chores/chores-01.md#feat-demo-cpu-pinned-placement-variants

- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Wait-policy hook + spin models: `_with(impl FnMut(u32)
   -> bool)` variants of alloc / producer / consumer
   reserve_slot (closure votes keep-waiting per retry), a
   shipped spin policy plus `_spin` wrappers as the model
   users copy, then measure all three forms (raw loop,
   closure, `_spin`) in iiac-perf to verify the seam is
   zero-cost [[11]].
2. Descriptor queue endpoints: paired DescSender (loan +
   send) / DescReceiver (recv) owning ring endpoint +
   registry access, so the demo's ~20-line send path
   becomes ~3 lines and `resolve`'s unsafe is audited once
   inside the crate (recv safe by construction); guard
   handed back on Full [[11]].
3. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Perf benches live in
  [iiac-perf](https://github.com/winksaville/iiac-perf)
  (sibling repo `../iiac-perf`), not here — its calibrated
  harness compares zc-ring against mpsc et al. directly
  (`zcring-1t`/`zcring-2t` mirroring `mpsc_1t`/`mpsc_2t`).
  An in-repo bench only if per-commit regression tracking
  proves necessary.

- Study [iceoryx2](https://github.com/eclipse-iceoryx/iceoryx2)
  before implementing message pools — battle-tested loan/send
  decoupling and pool-offset machinery; how it differs from
  this project in
  [Prior art: iceoryx2](ring-buffer-design.md#prior-art-iceoryx2).
- `#[global_allocator]` experiment over size-class pools:
  GlobalAlloc is `&self` + any-thread, so it needs
  shared-allocation pools (phase 2 gen-tagged head) or
  per-thread pools with a routing layer; arbitrary `Layout`
  needs size-class selection + an oversize fallback. Frees
  from any thread are already natural (MPSC push). Classic
  mempool → malloc arc; measure the object-pool form in
  iiac-perf first.
- Private per-handle cache in front of the shared free-stack
  (tcache-over-arenas): alloc/free hit a thread-private list
  with plain load/store; refill/flush moves batches to the
  CAS stack, amortizing one CAS over N messages. Motivating
  datum: 2 uncontended CAS = 8.7 ns of the pool's 9.9 ns
  single-thread round trip (vs malloc tcache's zero
  atomics). Hold until iiac-perf shows per-op CAS matters in
  a composed workload; we think the pool's tail latency
  (p99, stddev) already beats malloc — no arena locks, no
  brk/mmap — and that matters more than the mean.
- `Message` trait over the payload cast boilerplate: const
  `MSG_ID` + the zerocopy bounds; receiver-side dispatch
  (read tag, match, cast) without per-call-site ceremony,
  and maybe a transport seam so an embedded
  pointer-descriptor profile slots in behind the same API
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#descriptor-and-registry-design-070).
- BufSlot auto-free on Drop (RAII, iceoryx2-style): kills the
  silent leak-on-drop footgun at the cost of guard-type
  asymmetry (ring guards' drop = do-nothing) and a
  ManuallyDrop dance in free/send paths. Decide when
  descriptor-queue send lands — explicit free is easier to
  upgrade than to walk back.
- Overflow FIFO: when a queue's ring is Full, append the
  message to a sender-private intrusive pending list (same
  embedded next-link the free-stack uses; zero allocation,
  naturally bounded by pool capacity)
  [details](ring-buffer-design.md#overflow-fifo-future).
- Blocking layer above the crate (futex, eventfd, async
  wakers) built on the header's user line — mechanism and
  contracts in
  [Blocking and user words](ring-buffer-design.md#blocking-and-user-words);
  possibly a companion wrapper crate so independent peers
  share one protocol.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.
- Per-target / configurable `CACHE_LINE` (128 for Apple
  M-series false sharing, tiny for cache-less MCUs) — safe
  since attach validates the header's `cache_line`; decide
  values from iiac-perf measurements.
- Embedded floor: protocol is atomic load/store only (no
  CAS), so thumbv6m works today; keep it that way where
  possible (endpoint claims wants CAS — gate it), and 8/16-bit
  targets would need index-width genericization.
- Shared `Geometry` struct (`slot_size`, `capacity`, `mask`)
  held by Ring and passed whole to the endpoint
  constructors, slimming their signatures and Ring's fields.
- Black-box test split: move the public-API protocol tests
  (roundtrip, abandoned guards, threaded stress) to
  `tests/protocol.rs`; white-box tests (u32 wrap, attach
  header internals) stay in lib.rs. Do it when a trybuild
  compile-fail harness lands there too (pins the
  "second reserve_slot does not compile" guarantee).

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.
